original_abstract: Photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$) from water ($H_2O$) and dioxygen ($O_2$) represents a promising strategy for generating a sustainable solar fuel that can be easily stored and utilized in fuel cells. This study demonstrates the visible-light-driven production of $H_2O_2$ in acidic aqueous media by coupling a ruthenium-based photosensitizer, $[Ru^{II}(Me_2phen)_3]^{2+}$, with various water oxidation catalysts (WOCs). Under photoirradiation, the excited state of the ruthenium complex undergoes oxidative quenching by $O_2$ to generate a superoxide radical anion ($O_2^{\bullet–}$), which subsequently disproportionates to form $H_2O_2$. The resulting $[Ru^{III}(Me_2phen)_3]^{3+}$ species is then reduced back to its initial state by $H_2O$ with the assistance of $Ir(OH)_3$ nanoparticles acting as a WOC. To enhance performance, the addition of $Sc^{3+}$ as a Lewis acid was found to effectively inhibit the back electron transfer between $[Ru^{III}(Me_2phen)_3]^{3+}$ and $O_2^{\bullet–}$, as confirmed by transient absorption and EPR spectroscopy. Replacing the $Ir(OH)_3$ nanoparticles with a homogeneous cobalt complex, $[Co^{III}(Cp^*)(bpy)(H_2O)]^{2+}$, further optimized the system, yielding a quantum yield of 37% at 450 nm and a solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25%. These results provide a robust framework for the carbon-free production of $H_2O_2$ using solar energy and abundant chemical feedstocks.
humanized_abstract: Here we show that hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can be produced via solar-driven catalysis using water (H2O) and dioxygen (O2) as inexpensive and abundant resources, paving the way for sustainable solar fuel production. We have established a visible light-promoted photocatalytic process to produce H2O2 under acidic aqueous conditions by combining a Ru photosensitizer (complex [RuII(Me2phen)3]2+ with various water oxidation catalysts (WOCs). Upon photoexcitation the Ru complex oxidatively quenches O2 into the highly reactive superoxide radical anion (O2•–) and H2O2 is formed as the result of the subsequent disproportionation of O2•–, while the reduced complex [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ is re-reduced back by H2O using the added WOC—Ir(OH)3 nanoparticles. The addition of Sc3+ to the reaction media allows to suppress the back electron transfer between the reduced Ru complex and O2•–, as elucidated by transient absorption and EPR spectroscopy. By replacing the Ir(OH)3 nanoparticles as a WOC with a homogeneous Co complex—[CoIII(Cp*))(bpy)(H2O)]2+ we achieve a 37% quantum yield at 450 nm and a corresponding solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25% at neutral pH confirming the feasibility of producing H2O2 in a carbon-neutral manner using solar energy and abundant feedstocks.

The addition of Sc3+ to the reaction media allows to suppress the back electron transfer between the reduced Ru complex and O2•–, as elucidated by transient absorption and EPR spectroscopy. By replacing the Ir(OH)3 nanoparticles as a WOC with a homogeneous Co complex—[CoIII(Cp*))(bpy)(H2O)]2+ we achieve a 37% quantum yield at 450 nm and a corresponding solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25% at neutral pH confirming the feasibility of producing H2O2 in a carbon-neutral manner using solar energy and abundant feedstocks.
